one.zh: NULLITY – Where a decree of nullity is sought – Where the female respondent’s birth certificate from a foreign country contained an error of fact and …
one.en: NULLITY – Where a decree of nullity is sought – Where the female respondent’s birth certificate from a foreign country contained an error of fact and …

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – NULLITY – Where a decree of nullity is sought – Where the female respondent’s birth certificate from a foreign country contained an error of fact and listed her as male – Where there is no dispute that the respondent is biologically female and has had two children – Where the respondent’s failure to properly rectify the birth certificate meant that the marriage could not be recognised in the Philippines – Whether the inability to have the marriage recognised in the Philippines was a fraud that went to the applicant’s consent – Application dismissed.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

RIETHMULLER J:

The applicant seeks a declaration that the marriage between him and the respondent is void either because the respondent lacked legal capacity to marry, or that the applicant’s consent was obtained by fraud.

BACKGROUND

The parties married in Australia in 2019 and separated on a final basis on 25 January 2025. They have one child together, born in 2020, and the respondent has a child from a previous relationship.
The respondent did not participate in the nullity hearing.
The applicant’s affidavit sets out the facts and circumstances of the parties’ relationship. The parties met in 2018 on Facebook Messenger and communicated regularly. The applicant travelled to the Philippines, where the respondent was living, in March 2019, when they first met in person. The applicant, who is male, always understood that the respondent was female.
In 2019 the respondent fell pregnant and later gave birth to their son in 2020.
The applicant says that:
Prior to proposing marriage, I had decided that the Philippines was the country I wished to live in long-term. To do so, I understood I would need a 13A resident visa, which requires:
(a) a legally valid marriage to a Filipina woman; and

(b) registration of that marriage with the PSA [Philippine Statistics Authority].

This was a significant factor in my intention to marry the Respondent.

(Applicant’s affidavit filed 2 December 2025, paragraph 2.4)
In 2019 the parties married in Suburb G, New South Wales. For the marriage the respondent relied upon her Philippine passport, which described her as “Female”.
In November 2019 the respondent lodged an application for a Temporary Partner Visa (Subclass 820) relying upon her Philippine passport.
In 2021 the applicant says that the respondent provided him with a birth certificate (as part of the visa application process) which listed her as “Female”. He accepted the document at face value, no doubt because it accorded with his lived experience of an intimate relationship with the respondent and having had a child together.
The parties lived together in Australia until 2023. At this time the relationship was deteriorating. The applicant says that “In July 2023, I relocated temporarily to the Philippines for employment. Our son [X] travelled with me. The Respondent and her elder son remained in Australia at that time” (applicant’s affidavit filed 2 December 2025, paragraph 2.10). The respondent joined the applicant and their son later in 2023 in the Philippines. He says that by late 2023 the marriage had broken down, and he returned to Australia in August 2024. However, it appears that they did not finally separate until 25 January 2025 when the respondent told the applicant that the marriage was over.

The parties’ intentions with respect to residence

The applicant says that:
From early 2019, prior to meeting [the respondent] in person and throughout our developing relationship, I had already formed the clear intention that I wished to eventually reside in the Philippines on a permanent or long-term basis. The Philippines was a country I had already travelled to multiple times, and I had begun researching various visa pathways available to foreign nationals seeking residence.

(Applicant’s affidavit filed 2 December 2025, paragraph 2.14)
He later stated:
On 7 April 2019 (Annexure N-1), [the respondent] and I discussed my desire to remain in the Philippines long-term. I said: "If you can't get the visa to stay here in Australia then I will stay in the Philippines with you." This statement reflects my clear intention to relocate to the Philippines if necessary, contingent upon a lawful marriage.

(Applicant’s affidavit filed 2 December 2025, paragraph 3.12)
The applicant says that he and the respondent had discussed a desire to reside together in the Philippines and the practical issues concerning visas, including the need to obtain a married spouse visa on the basis of him marrying the respondent. During these discussions the respondent disclosed that there was an error on her birth certificate. Early in his affidavit (at paragraph 3.7) the applicant says he did not know the error was as to her sex, nor the difficulties this may create in Philippines law, however later said:
Prior to marriage, in March 2019, she admitted to me through Messenger that her [Philippine Statistics Authority birth certificate] listed her sex as "Male" and that she needed to have it corrected.

(Applicant’s affidavit filed 2 December 2025, paragraph 6.3)
The respondent set out her plans to resolve the issue, which do not appear to have been entirely formal. She later provided a new passport which led the applicant to believe she had resolved the difficulties caused by the error in her birth registration.
The applicant summarises his case in his affidavit, saying:
2.18 My willingness to commit to marriage with [the respondent], relocate permanently to the Philippines, and structure my long-term financial planning accordingly, was predicated upon the belief – induced by [the respondent’s] statements, omissions, and conduct – that she was a biologically and legally recognised female in all respects required under Philippine law for a valid marriage.

...

3.10 ... I would not have married [the respondent] if I had known:
(i) that her [Philippine Statistics Authority birth certificate] still lists her as "Male" (Annexure A-2, Annexure A-1);

(ii) that any marriage to her is not recognised in the Philippines (Annexure A-5, Annexure F-1);

(iii) that her passports listing "Female" (Annexure B-1, Annexure B-2) were inconsistent with her [Philippine Statistics Authority] record; and

(iv) that her 2019 passport was issued after a fixer-assisted [birth certificate] alteration attempt that was never legally registered.

(Applicant’s affidavit filed 2 December 2025)
The applicant says that the marriage of the parties in Australia will not be recognised in the Philippines as the respondent (whilst in fact biologically female) is listed as “Male” on her birth certificate. However, there is no expert evidence in this regard.

GROUNDS

Section 23B of the Marriage Act 1961 (Cth) sets out which marriages are void:
23B Grounds on which marriages are void
(1) A marriage to which this Division applies that takes place after the commencement of section 13 of the Marriage Amendment Act 1985 is void where:
(a) either of the parties is, at the time of the marriage, lawfully married to some other person;
(b) the parties are within a prohibited relationship;

...

(d) the consent of either of the parties is not a real consent because:
(i) it was obtained by duress or fraud;

(ii) that party is mistaken as to the identity of the other party or as to the nature of the ceremony performed; or

(iii) that party did not understand the nature and effect of the marriage ceremony; or

(e) either of the parties is not of marriageable age;

and not otherwise.
...
The marriage took place in Australia. Neither party was married to another person, nor within a prohibited relationship. Both were well over the minimum marriageable age.
At the time of the marriage the law did not require the parties to have different sexes for a lawful marriage. Even if the law in Australia required the marriage to be a union of a man and a woman it would not have invalidated the marriage in this case. As the respondent is in fact biologically female, her Philippines birth certificate would not be conclusive evidence of her sex under Australian law.
The fact that a marriage in Australia may not be recognised by a foreign country does not invalidate the marriage under Australian law; it simply means that the marriage is not recognised in the foreign country.
The only relevant provision is s 23B(1)(d) which relates to whether the applicant’s consent was “real”. There was no issue of duress raised in the proceedings.
Hence, the only real ground was that of fraud in s 23B(1)(d)(i). The applicant says that he was unaware that the difficulties with the respondent’s Philippines birth certificate would prevent them from being recognised as a married couple in the Philippines and thus impede him in obtaining residence rights in the Philippines. He also complains that the respondent had obtained a false passport to show her correct sex and that when he had given her money to rectify the difficulty with her Philippines birth certificate she had not applied it to the legal course necessary to seek a rectification of the birth certificate. None of these matters are a fraud that goes to his or her consent to marry. Their respective consents were real, however her ‘qualities’ differed from what he expected as the marriage to her would not lead to citizenship in the Philippines.
On the traditional interpretation of the fraud provision even misrepresentation as to the qualities of a person are insufficient to result in a lack of “real consent”. For example, concealing a previous divorce was insufficient (Gani & Drasha [2017] FamCA 475), as was concealing the fact that they had previously had children with another: Aird v Hamilton–Reid [2007] FamCA 4; (2007) 208 FLR 46. Failure to disclose a diagnosis of AIDS has been held to be insufficient to show fraud in the relevant sense (Rick & King [2011] FamCAFC 220) and even a lie by the husband that he was dying of cancer to induce the wife to marry was insufficient: Zacharia & Paradisio [2008] FamCA 688.
In this case the applicant was aware of the difficulties with the respondent’s Philippines birth certificate and that steps were required to resolve it. However, even if the respondent falsely claimed that she had resolved that difficulty it is difficult to see how it went to whether the applicant’s consent to the marriage was real.
There was no mistake as to the respondent’s identity: the applicant married the person he believed he was marrying.
There is no suggestion that the applicant did not understand the nature and effect of the ceremony.
I therefore dismiss the application for a declaration that the marriage is void.
The applicant sought a divorce in the event that the nullity application was dismissed. There is no doubt that the parties have now been separated for over 12 months and thus he would be entitled to a divorce should an application be made. A previous divorce application was dismissed after a hearing in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) on the basis that the parties had not been separated for 12 months at the time that application was made to the court. In the absence of a pending divorce application I am not persuaded that it is appropriate to make a divorce order.
The application will therefore be dismissed.
